g.text: Figure 1-1: Microarchitecture Latency vs. Working Set Size (Intel x86-64)

div.p: Working sets exceeding L3 capacity (LLC) induce 'Pointer Chasing' stalls. Memory wall effects trigger ~10x performance degradation as the CPU waits on DRAM row-buffer activation and CAS latency cycles.

g.title: ### L1 Data Cache (L1D) - **Latency:** 4 cycles (Fixed Load-to-Use) - **Throughput:** Two 32-byte loads per cycle - **Associativity:** 8-way set associative - **Line Size:** 64 Bytes

### L2 Unified Cache (MLC) - **Latency:** ~12 cycles (Non-inclusive) - **Bandwidth:** 32-64 bytes per cycle to L1 - **Associativity:** 4-way to 16-way - **Inclusive:** Depends on architecture (e.g., Skylake vs Sunny Cove)

### Last Level Cache (LLC) - **Latency:** 38-44 cycles (Architecture dependent) - **Structure:** Slice-based, ring/mesh interconnect - **Policy:** Inclusive (traditional Intel) or Non-inclusive (Server)

### Main Memory (DRAM) - **Latency:** ~200-350 cycles - **Components:** Memory Controller (iMC) + DIMM + Row Buffers - **Floor:** CAS Latency + Command Overhead - **NUMA:** Latency doubles for remote sockets
